claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 12 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 4 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility rules depend on the child's age:
- Children **under 16** typically qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally do not qualify

Please tell me your child's age.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about three of my children - Alex who's 7, Blake who's 12, and Charlie who's 10. Should I go through them one at a time or can you check all three together?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! That's very helpful. Since Alex (7), Blake (12), and Charlie (10) are all under 16 years old, they fall into the same age category, which means they follow the same initial eligibility path.

I can assess all three of them together as we go through the questions, since the rules will apply similarly to each of them. This will be more efficient for you.

Let me continue with the assessment for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) working 24 hours or more per week in paid work?**

This is quite rare for children of these ages (7, 10, and 12), but I need to confirm that none of them are working full-time (24+ hours per week).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them work at all - they're all in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected for children of their ages. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are any of Alex, Blake, or Charlie receiving benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in their own name (not as part of your family claim).

Again, this is uncommon for children of their ages, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are any of your children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) in an apprenticeship?**

Since they're all in primary school, I'm assuming the answer is no, but I need to confirm this to proceed correctly.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not in any apprenticeships - they're too young for that, they're just in regular primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's correct! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about your residency status, as this is an important eligibility requirement.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If yes, that typically satisfies the residency requirement. 

However, if you don't normally live in the UK, you might still qualify through other routes such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Other special circumstances

What is your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK - I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad, you may still qualify through certain special circumstances. Let me ask you some follow-up questions:

**Do any of these situations apply to you?**

1. **Are you a Crown servant** (or married to/in a civil partnership with a Crown servant) living abroad?

2. **Do you have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme?

3. **Do you have pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme? If so, are you:
   - Working and earning above the National Insurance threshold?
   - Actively looking for work (as a jobseeker)?
   - A student with sufficient resources?
   - Have sufficient resources to support yourself and your family without claiming certain benefits?
   - A family member of an EEA or Swiss national with right to reside?

4. **Are you living in certain qualifying countries** (such as Gibraltar or as part of specific arrangements)?

Please let me know which, if any, of these situations apply to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I'm just living abroad with my family - I'm not a Crown servant, I don't have any settled or pre-settled status, and I'm not in any of those special circumstances you mentioned.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, I need to let you know the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for Alex, Blake, or Charlie at this time.

**Reason:** You do not meet the UK residency requirements.

**Why this matters:** To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Being a Crown servant (or family member of one) living abroad
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme with qualifying criteria
  - Living in certain qualifying countries

Since you're living abroad and none of the special circumstances apply to you, you don't meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

**What you should know:**
- If your circumstances change and you return to live in the UK, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time
- If you become a Crown servant or your immigration status changes, you may also become eligible

**Reference:** You can find more information about eligibility at [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: If you have any questions about what might change your eligibility in the future, or if you'd like clarification on anything, please feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex (age 7) meets the age requirement (under 16), is not working full-time, does not receive benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, the claim for Alex is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

Blake (age 12) meets the age requirement (under 16), is not working full-time, does not receive benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, the claim for Blake is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

Charlie (age 10) meets the age requirement (under 16), is not working full-time, does not receive benefits in their own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, the claim for Charlie is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has three children (Alex age 7, Blake age 12, and Charlie age 10) who all meet the child-related eligibility criteria: they are all under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in disqualifying apprenticeships. However, the claimant is living abroad and does not meet any of the UK residency requirements or special circumstances exceptions (such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). As a result, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit for all three children due to failing the residency requirement.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
